title: OT Security Add-on Health and Configuration Status
spl:
index=_internal source=*scheduler.log status=failed savedsearch_name="OT*"
| stats count by savedsearch_name, status
| append [
    | rest /servicesNS/-/-/saved/searches search="OT*"
    | where disabled=1
    | table title disabled
  ]
| table savedsearch_name, title, status, disabled, count
description: Ensures OT Security Add-on correlation searches, lookups, and MITRE mappings stay healthy so process-safety detections do not silently fail.
value: Ensures OT Security Add-on correlation searches, lookups, and MITRE mappings stay healthy so process-safety detections do not silently fail.
grandmaExplanation: We help you make sure OT Security correlation searches, lookups, and known attack techniques mappings stay healthy so process-safety detections do not silently fail — so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: Deploy Splunk Enterprise Security and the OT Security Add-on. Schedule a daily check for disabled or failing correlation searches. Alert when any OT-prefixed saved search has failed or been disabled.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_internal` scheduler/search logs, `index=ot_security` `sourcetype="ot:security:health"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Deploy Splunk Enterprise Security and the OT Security Add-on. Schedule a daily check for disabled or failing correlation searches. Alert when any OT-prefixed saved search has failed or been disabled.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal source=*scheduler.log status=failed savedsearch_name="OT*"
| stats count by savedsearch_name, status
| append [
    | rest /servicesNS/-/-/saved/searches search="OT*"
    | where disabled=1
    | table title disabled
  ]
| table savedsearch_name, title, status, disabled, count
```

Understanding this SPL

**OT Security Add-on Health and Configuration Status** — Ensures OT Security Add-on correlation searches, lookups, and MITRE mappings stay healthy so process-safety detections do not silently fail.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_internal` scheduler/search logs, `index=ot_security` `sourcetype="ot:security:health"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_internal. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by savedsearch_name, status** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Appends rows from a subsearch with `append`.
• Pipeline stage (see **OT Security Add-on Health and Configuration Status**): table savedsearch_name, title, status, disabled, count


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (search name, status, last failure), Single value (failed OT searches count).
visualization: Table (search name, status, last failure), Single value (failed OT searches count).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
